refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks specificity on measurement boundaries, network assumptions, and safety context. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (sensor-to-actuator), establishes the percentile metric (p95), and anchors to the safety criticality constraint. This enables testable acceptance criteria and traceability to the Safety Manager and Tele-operator stakeholders.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct latency measurement under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles) using instrumented telemetry and tasking loops. Measure from sensor acquisition timestamp to actuator command execution. Collect 1000+ samples across representative workload scenarios and verify p95 ≤ 150 ms. Document network profile assumptions and any deviations.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and transmit vehicle state telemetry with bounded latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive and execute tele-operator tasking commands with bounded latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and communicate safety-critical alerts with latency compatible with emergency response.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall operate within agreed network conditions without exceeding p95 latency threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dedicated low-latency network path with priority queuing and edge-local processing to minimize round-trip delay.
DP-B: Implement predictive state estimation and command buffering to mask network latency from operator perception.
DP-C: Implement hybrid architecture with local autonomy fallback and asynchronous telemetry streaming to decouple latency-critical paths.
DP-D: Implement real-time middleware with deterministic scheduling and network optimization (compression, batching) to bound latency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement real-time middleware with deterministic scheduling and network optimization (compression, batching) to bound latency, combined with DP-A's dedicated low-latency network path and priority queuing to ensure p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed conditions.

potential_sources: Network path design (DP-A) is independent of middleware scheduling (DP-D); both are necessary and non-redundant.
Compression and batching (DP-D) may couple with telemetry fidelity requirements; mitigation via adaptive compression based on safety context.
Priority queuing (DP-A) couples with safety alert routing (FR-NGHE-008.3); SafetyAlert interface must have highest priority.
coupling_check.notes: The combination of deterministic middleware + dedicated network path is a well-established pattern in safety-critical teleoperation (e.g., surgical robotics, remote mining). Coupling is manageable through interface discipline and priority policies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are stable and predictable within agreed profile (latency, jitter, packet loss).
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and tele-operator endpoints have sufficient compute capacity for real-time middleware and compression.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed profile, p95 latency may exceed 150 ms; mitigation requires fallback to local autonomy or operation suspension.
RISK: Compression artifacts may degrade telemetry quality for safety-critical perception; mitigation via lossless compression for safety-critical channels.
RISK: Deterministic scheduling overhead may consume significant CPU; mitigation via hardware acceleration or edge processing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Explicitly define 'agreed network conditions' in a separate Network Profile specification (bandwidth, latency baseline, jitter, packet loss, availability SLA). Decompose DP-D into sub-DPs for middleware scheduling, compression strategy, and batching policy. Add acceptance criterion: 'System shall gracefully degrade to local autonomy if network latency exceeds 200 ms for >5 seconds.'

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency budget, including tele-operator command ingestion, vehicle state telemetry acquisition, real-time middleware scheduling, network optimization (compression, batching), and command execution. It interfaces with Vehicle for sensor/actuator access, Safety Services for alert routing, and Network Link for transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings) transmitted from Teleoperation to tele-operator console; latency-critical, may be compressed.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Tele-operator commands (movement, tool control) received by Teleoperation from remote console; latency-critical, prioritized.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts (collision detection, emergency stop) transmitted from Teleoperation to Safety Services and tele-operator; highest priority, minimal latency.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (5G, satellite, terrestrial) managed by Teleoperation for latency optimization; includes priority queuing and congestion management.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end latency. All latency-critical logic (middleware, compression, scheduling) is contained. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link interface is external dependency.

Vehicle owns sensor/actuator hardware but not teleoperation logic. If latency responsibility assigned to Vehicle, it would require Vehicle to implement middleware, compression, and network optimization—inappropriate diffusion of teleoperation concerns into vehicle domain.

Safety Services owns safety policy and alert generation, not teleoperation latency. Assigning latency to Safety Services would couple safety logic with network optimization—poor separation of concerns.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but not the operational owner. Assigning latency to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple sub-modules without clear accountability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because it encapsulates the end-to-end latency path: it receives Tasking input, orchestrates Vehicle telemetry acquisition, applies real-time middleware and network optimization, and transmits Telemetry and SafetyAlert outputs. All latency-critical logic is contained within this boundary.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become a performance bottleneck if not properly architected; mitigation via multi-threaded design, hardware acceleration, or edge processing.

RISK: Network Link interface is external and uncontrolled; if network conditions degrade, Teleoperation cannot guarantee p95 ≤ 150 ms; mitigation via fallback to local autonomy and graceful degradation policy.

RISK: Vehicle sensor latency (acquisition + transmission to Teleoperation) is not owned by Teleoperation; if Vehicle sensors are slow, end-to-end latency will exceed budget; mitigation via Vehicle-level latency requirements and interface specification.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules: (1) Command Ingestion & Execution (owns Tasking interface), (2) Telemetry Acquisition & Compression (owns Telemetry interface), (3) Real-Time Middleware (owns scheduling and buffering), (4) Network Optimization (owns Network Link interface). Establish latency budget allocation across sub-modules. Define interface contracts with Vehicle and Safety Services to specify sensor/actuator latency contributions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (real-time middleware + dedicated network path + priority queuing) directly addresses the FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. The DP is implementable, testable, and grounded in established teleoperation practices. Assumptions about network conditions and compute capacity are explicit and manageable.

diffusion_risks: Network Link interface is external; Teleoperation cannot fully control network latency. Mitigation: establish Network Profile specification and fallback policies.
Vehicle sensor latency is not owned by Teleoperation; if Vehicle is slow, end-to-end latency will exceed budget. Mitigation: allocate latency budget to Vehicle and establish interface SLAs.
Safety Services alert routing may introduce latency if not prioritized. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must have highest priority in Teleoperation middleware.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module contains all latency-critical logic (middleware, compression, scheduling, priority queuing). External dependencies (Vehicle, Safety Services, Network Link) are well-defined via interfaces. Containment is strong; diffusion is limited to necessary external dependencies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Measurement points: (1) Tasking ingestion timestamp, (2) Vehicle sensor acquisition timestamp, (3) Telemetry transmission timestamp, (4) SafetyAlert transmission timestamp, (5) Actuator command execution timestamp. Collect p95 statistics across 1000+ samples.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency budget (p95 ≤ 150 ms). Sub-allocations to Vehicle (sensor latency), Network Link (transmission latency), and Safety Services (alert routing latency) are defined via interface contracts. Ownership and responsibility are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Define Network Profile specification (bandwidth, latency baseline, jitter, packet loss, availability SLA) as a separate artifact. This is a critical assumption for the DP and must be explicit.

Establish latency budget allocation across Teleoperation sub-modules and external dependencies (Vehicle, Network Link, Safety Services). Example: Teleoperation middleware 30 ms, Vehicle sensors 40 ms, Network transmission 50 ms, Safety Services 10 ms, margin 20 ms.
Define graceful degradation policy: if network latency exceeds 200 ms for >5 seconds, system shall transition to local autonomy or suspend remote operation. This is a safety-critical requirement.

Specify compression strategy for Telemetry: lossless for safety-critical channels (collision detection, emergency stop), lossy for non-critical channels (video, detailed sensor data). This mitigates risk of compression artifacts affecting safety.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and the selected DP is sound, but the FR is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The four sub-FRs (telemetry acquisition, tasking execution, safety alert communication, network operation) are distinct concerns that require separate DPs and IMs. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership, interface contracts, and latency budget allocation. The Teleoperation IM assignment is appropriate and ready for sub-module decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and transmit vehicle state telemetry with bounded latency ≤ 50 ms (allocated budget).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive and execute tele-operator tasking commands with bounded latency ≤ 40 ms (allocated budget).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and communicate safety-critical alerts with latency ≤ 10 ms (allocated budget).

FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall operate within agreed network conditions (latency baseline, jitter, packet loss) without exceeding p95 latency threshold; shall gracefully degrade to local autonomy if network latency exceeds 200 ms for >5 seconds.

assumptions: Network conditions are stable and predictable within agreed profile (latency, jitter, packet loss).
Vehicle and tele-operator endpoints have sufficient compute capacity for real-time middleware and compression.
Vehicle sensor latency is ≤ 40 ms (to be validated via Vehicle-level requirements).
Network transmission latency is ≤ 50 ms under agreed conditions (to be validated via Network Profile specification).
Safety Services alert routing introduces ≤ 10 ms latency (to be validated via Safety Services interface contract).
Tele-operator can safely operate with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms (to be validated via human factors analysis and safety case).
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed profile, causing p95 latency to exceed 150 ms. Mitigation: establish fallback to local autonomy and operation suspension policy.
Compression artifacts may degrade telemetry quality for safety-critical perception. Mitigation: use lossless compression for safety-critical channels.
Deterministic scheduling overhead may consume significant CPU. Mitigation: hardware acceleration or edge processing.
Vehicle sensor latency may exceed allocated budget. Mitigation: establish Vehicle-level latency requirements and interface SLAs.
Safety Services alert routing may introduce unexpected latency. Mitigation: prioritize SafetyAlert interface in Teleoperation middleware.
evidence_needed: Network Profile specification: agreed bandwidth, latency baseline, jitter, packet loss, availability SLA.
Vehicle-level latency requirements: sensor acquisition and transmission latency ≤ 40 ms.
Safety Services interface contract: alert routing latency ≤ 10 ms.
Real-time middleware design: scheduling algorithm, compression strategy, priority queuing policy.
Latency measurement plan: instrumentation points, sampling strategy, statistical analysis (p95 calculation).
Human factors analysis: operator comfort and safety with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms.
Safety case: justification that p95 latency ≤ 150 ms is sufficient for safe remote operation under agreed conditions.
